traitSemantics:
All CRUD traits build their routes on top of the controller's class-level @RequestMapping base path; they contribute mapping methods via Java interface default methods (Spring registers the inherited @GetMapping/@PostMapping/@PutMapping/@DeleteMapping from the implemented interface). They all read config from getRestCrudConfigBuilder() (repository, dtoClass, entityClass, supportedOperations) which the implementing controller must supply.

WithReadRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D>: adds GET (base path, no sub-path) -> findAll() returning List<D> (every entity via repository.findAll(), converted to DTO, passed through beforeRequestEnd + filterGetResult), and GET "{id}" -> findOneById(@PathVariable N id) returning D (repository findByIdOrDie -> DTO). Both gated by supportedOperations.canReadAll() (throws AccessDenied otherwise).

WithDeleteRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D>: adds DELETE "{id}" -> delete(@PathVariable N id) returning ResponseEntity<Void> 204 NO_CONTENT; calls repository.deleteById(id); gated by supportedOperations.canDeleteAny().

CrudRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D> (extends CrudRestServiceNoOpEventsTrait + WithReadRestServiceTrait + WithDeleteRestServiceTrait): inherits the 2 GET reads and the DELETE, and itself adds POST (base path) -> saveNew(@RequestBody D) returning D (entityFromDto, must NOT have an id, doSave=repository.save, gated by canCreate()), and PUT "{id}" -> saveExisting(@PathVariable N id, @RequestBody D) returning D (id must be present and match path, repository.save, gated by canUpdateAny()). So a controller implementing only CrudRestServiceTrait exposes: GET '', GET '{id}', POST '', PUT '{id}', DELETE '{id}'.

CrudWithImprovementsRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D> (extends CrudRestServiceTrait): inherits all 5 basic CRUD routes plus adds improvement sub-routes: GET '{id}/improvement' -> find returning ImprovementDto (entity.getImprovement); PUT '{id}/improvement' -> saveImprovement(@RequestBody ImprovementDto) returning ImprovementDto (ImprovementBo.createOrUpdateFromDto + repository.save); GET '{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements' -> findUnitTypeImprovements returning List<ImprovementUnitTypeDto> (ImprovementUnitTypeBo.loadImprovementUnitTypes); POST '{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements' -> addUnitTypeImprovement(@RequestBody ImprovementUnitTypeDto) returning ImprovementUnitTypeDto (ImprovementUnitTypeBo.add + ImprovementRepository.save); DELETE '{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements/{unitTypeImprovementId}' -> deleteUnitTypeImprovement returning ResponseEntity<Void> 204 (ImprovementUnitTypeBo.removeImprovementUnitType). It also overrides beforeSave (auto-creates an Improvement row if none) and beforeRequestEnd (nulls the improvement on the returned DTO).

CrudWithRequirementsRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D> (extends CrudRestServiceNoOpEventsTrait only; requires getObject() returning ObjectEnum): adds GET '{id}/requirements' -> findRequirements(@PathVariable N id) returning List<RequirementInformationDto> (RequirementInformationBo.findRequirements); POST '{id}/requirements' -> addRequirement(@PathVariable N id, @RequestBody RequirementInformationDto) returning RequirementInformationDto (RequirementBo.addRequirementFromDto); DELETE '{id}/requirements/{requirementInformationId}' -> deleteRequirement returning ResponseEntity<Void> 204 (RequirementInformationBo.delete). It does NOT by itself add the basic create/read/update/delete CRUD routes. It also overrides filterGetResult to support query-param requirement filtering (filterByRequirementName/SecondValue/ThirdValue) on findAll results.

CrudWithFullRestService<N,E,R,D> (extends CrudWithRequirementsRestServiceTrait + CrudWithImprovementsRestServiceTrait + WithReadRestServiceTrait + CrudRestServiceTrait + WithDeleteRestServiceTrait): the union of all the above. Concrete routes: GET '' (findAll), GET '{id}' (findOneById), POST '' (saveNew), PUT '{id}' (saveExisting), DELETE '{id}' (delete), plus the improvement block (GET/PUT '{id}/improvement', GET/POST '{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements', DELETE '{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements/{unitTypeImprovementId}'), plus the requirements block (GET/POST '{id}/requirements', DELETE '{id}/requirements/{requirementInformationId}'). beforeRequestEnd is resolved to the improvements variant.

CrudWithRequirementGroupsRestServiceTrait (not in the asked list but present): adds GET/POST '{id}/requirement-group', DELETE '{id}/requirement-group/{groupId}', POST '{id}/requirement-group/{requirementGroupId}/requirement', DELETE '{id}/requirement-group/{requirementGroupId}/requirement/{requirementInformationId}'.

WithImageRestServiceTrait<N,E,D,R> (not asked but present): adds NO HTTP routes; it only overrides beforeSave to resolve the image id into an ImageStore entity (ImageStoreBo.findByIdOrDie) before saving. It is a behavior mixin, not a route provider.

CrudRestServiceNoOpEventsTrait<D,E> and BaseRestServiceTrait: add NO routes; they provide lifecycle hooks (beforeConversion/beforeSave/afterSave/beforeRequestEnd/filterGetResult) and helper checks (checkPost, checkMapEntry) respectively.

Note: among the asked traits, none of the game controllers in rest/game actually implement CrudRestServiceTrait, CrudWithFullRestService, CrudWithImprovementsRestServiceTrait, or CrudWithRequirementsRestServiceTrait — those are used by admin controllers. The only CRUD trait used in rest/game is WithReadRestServiceTrait (TimeSpecialRestService), which there contributes GET 'game/time_special' and GET 'game/time_special/{id}'.

traitSemantics:
All CRUD traits build their routes relative to the controller's @RequestMapping base path; the entity id type N is the path variable.

WithReadRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D>: adds GET "" -> List<D> findAll() (gated by canReadAll) and GET "{id}" -> D findOneById (gated by canReadAll). DTO type D is the controller's *Dto.

WithDeleteRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D>: adds DELETE "{id}" -> ResponseEntity<Void> delete (gated by canDeleteAny), repository.deleteById.

CrudRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D>: extends WithRead + WithDelete, and adds POST "" -> D saveNew(@RequestBody D) (gated by canCreate) and PUT "{id}" -> D saveExisting(@PathVariable N id, @RequestBody D) (gated by canUpdateAny). So implementing CrudRestServiceTrait alone yields the full standard set: GET "", GET "{id}", POST "", PUT "{id}", DELETE "{id}".

CrudWithImprovementsRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D> (extends CrudRestServiceTrait): adds the standard CRUD set PLUS improvement sub-resource routes: GET "{id}/improvement" -> ImprovementDto, PUT "{id}/improvement" (@RequestBody ImprovementDto) -> ImprovementDto, GET "{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements" -> List<ImprovementUnitTypeDto>, POST "{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements" (@RequestBody ImprovementUnitTypeDto) -> ImprovementUnitTypeDto, DELETE "{id}/improvement/unitTypeImprovements/{unitTypeImprovementId}" -> ResponseEntity<Void>.

CrudWithRequirementsRestServiceTrait<N,E,R,D> (requires getObject() returning an ObjectEnum): adds GET "{id}/requirements" -> List<RequirementInformationDto>, POST "{id}/requirements" (@RequestBody RequirementInformationDto) -> RequirementInformationDto, DELETE "{id}/requirements/{requirementInformationId}" -> ResponseEntity<Void>. Does NOT itself add base CRUD (it extends only the no-op events trait), but also adds query-param-based filtering (filterByRequirementName/SecondValue/ThirdValue) to the inherited findAll.

CrudWithRequirementGroupsRestServiceTrait<E,R,D> (id type fixed to Integer; requires getObject()): adds GET "{id}/requirement-group" -> List<RequirementGroupDto>, POST "{id}/requirement-group" (@RequestBody RequirementGroupDto) -> RequirementGroupDto, DELETE "{id}/requirement-group/{groupId}" -> ResponseEntity<Void>, POST "{id}/requirement-group/{requirementGroupId}/requirement" (@RequestBody RequirementInformationDto) -> RequirementInformationDto, DELETE "{id}/requirement-group/{requirementGroupId}/requirement/{requirementInformationId}" -> ResponseEntity<Void>. Does not add base CRUD itself.

CrudWithFullRestService<N,E,R,D>: composite = CrudWithRequirements + CrudWithImprovements + WithRead + CrudRestServiceTrait + WithDelete. So it yields the full standard CRUD set (GET "", GET "{id}", POST "", PUT "{id}", DELETE "{id}") PLUS all improvement routes PLUS all requirements routes. Requires getObject().

WithImageRestServiceTrait<N,E,D,R>: adds NO HTTP routes of its own; it only overrides beforeSave to resolve the image id into an ImageStore on save. Purely a save-time hook mixed into the CRUD POST/PUT.

Auth scope is by package: every controller under rest/admin is authScope=admin; every controller under rest/open is authScope=open. There is no rest/game controller in the two scanned directories.

rustTypeMapping:
MySQL -> sqlx/Rust type mapping cheat sheet (sqlx MySql driver, with chrono feature):

BOOLEAN / SEMANTIC:
- tinyint(1)            -> bool         (sqlx maps MySQL TINYINT(1) to bool). NOTE: several "flag" columns in this schema are declared as plain `tinyint` (no display width) or `tinyint UNSIGNED`, which sqlx decodes as i8/u8, NOT bool. Decide per-column: if it is a 0/1 flag, decode as i8 then cast, or change the column to tinyint(1). Affected flag-ish columns declared as bare tinyint: missions.resolved, missions.invisible, mission_types.is_shared, obtained_units.is_from_capture, obtained_upgrades.available, units.is_unique (tinyint UNSIGNED), units.bypass_shield, units.cloned_improvements, upgrades... (upgrades.cloned_improvements is tinyint(1)), factions.hidden/cloned_improvements, special_locations.cloned_improvements, speed_impact_groups.is_fixed, attack_rule_entries.can_attack, websocket_messages_status.unwhiling_to_delivery/socket_server_ack/socket_not_found, admin_users.can_add_admins (tinyint UNSIGNED), planets.home (tinyint), improvements.more_missions_value (tinyint, signed small int really).

INTEGERS (signed):
- tinyint               -> i8
- smallint              -> i16
- mediumint             -> i32  (sqlx has no i24; use i32)
- int / integer         -> i32
- bigint                -> i64

INTEGERS (UNSIGNED) - sqlx maps to the unsigned Rust type:
- tinyint UNSIGNED      -> u8
- smallint UNSIGNED     -> u16
- mediumint UNSIGNED    -> u32  (no u24; use u32)
- int UNSIGNED          -> u32
- bigint UNSIGNED       -> u64
  IMPORTANT: most surrogate PKs/FKs here are UNSIGNED (smallint/int/bigint UNSIGNED) -> u16/u32/u64. But user-facing ids are inconsistent: user_storage.id is signed `int` (-> i32) while it is referenced by FKs typed `int` (signed) too; planet ids are bigint UNSIGNED (-> u64) but missions.source_planet/target_planet are signed `bigint` (-> i64) even though they reference planets.id (bigint UNSIGNED). Keep Rust struct field types matching the actual COLUMN type, not the referenced PK, to avoid sqlx decode errors. requirements.id / requirements_information.id are signed `smallint` (-> i16) unlike most other ids.

FLOAT / DOUBLE / DECIMAL:
- float                 -> f32
- float UNSIGNED        -> f32 (sqlx still decodes as f32)
- double                -> f64
- double UNSIGNED       -> f64
- decimal(13,4)         -> rust_decimal::Decimal (enable sqlx `bigdecimal`/`rust_decimal` feature) or f64 if precision loss acceptable. Only in qrtz_simprop_triggers.

DATE / TIME:
- datetime              -> chrono::NaiveDateTime (no tz). For nullable -> Option<NaiveDateTime>. Columns DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP (user_storage.last_action) still decode as NaiveDateTime.
- timestamp             -> chrono::NaiveDateTime (MySQL TIMESTAMP is UTC-normalized; can also map to chrono::DateTime<Utc> via the `chrono` feature). obtained_unit_temporal_information.expiration is `timestamp NOT NULL`.
- timestamp(6)          -> chrono::NaiveDateTime with microsecond precision (scheduled_tasks.* ). Nullable timestamp(6) NULL -> Option<NaiveDateTime>.
- date                  -> chrono::NaiveDate (sponsors.expiration_date, nullable -> Option<NaiveDate>).

STRINGS / TEXT / ENUM / BLOB:
- varchar / char        -> String (Option<String> when nullable). char(N) is space-trimmed-on-read in MySQL.
- text / mediumtext / longtext / tinytext -> String. (tinytext columns websocket_messages_status.web_browser_ack/is_user_ack_required are mislabeled flags but typed tinytext -> String.)
- enum(...)             -> sqlx maps to String by default. For type safety derive a Rust enum with #[derive(sqlx::Type)] #[sqlx(type_name = "...", rename_all="...")] or just read as String and parse. Enums present: active_time_specials.state, audit.action, *_target ('UNIT','UNIT_TYPE'), improvements_unit_types.type, speed_impact_groups/unit_types can_* ('NONE','OWNED_ONLY','ANY'), sponsors.type, suspicions.source, tutorial_sections_entries.event.
- blob                  -> Vec<u8> (Option<Vec<u8>> when nullable). scheduled_tasks.task_data, qrtz_* BLOB/JOB_DATA/CALENDAR columns.

NULLABILITY: wrap every column marked nullable (no NOT NULL, or DEFAULT NULL) in Option<T>. Columns with a non-null DEFAULT are still NOT NULL unless explicitly nullable.

AUTO_INCREMENT: PK ids are DB-generated; in INSERT structs make them Option or use a separate insert struct without the id and read last_insert_id().

GOTCHAS specific to this schema:
- Mixed signedness on logical FKs (planets.id u64 vs missions.*_planet i64; obtained_units source/target_planet are bigint UNSIGNED u64). Match the literal column.
- objects.description (varchar PK) and configuration.name (varchar PK) are string PKs.
- Composite PKs: planet_list(user_id,planet_id), websocket_events_information(event_name,user_id), scheduled_tasks(task_name,task_instance), all qrtz_* tables. Use them as composite keys; sqlx has no entity layer so just map columns.
- Charset varies (latin1, utf8, utf8mb4, ascii) but all decode to Rust String regardless.
- `see_orphan_relations` is a VIEW, not a table (read-only); query it like a table but cannot INSERT/UPDATE.

# OWGE Websocket / Socket.IO Realtime System — Investigation Report

## Overview & topology

OWGE's realtime channel is a **netty-socketio** server (library `com.corundumstudio.socketio`), not Spring STOMP. It runs as a **separate TCP server inside the `game-rest` JVM**, distinct from the HTTP/REST listener. It is configured in `/public/owge/business/src/main/java/com/kevinguanchedarias/owgejava/configurations/WebsocketConfiguration.java`, which extends the socketio `Configuration` class and binds host `OWGE_WS_HOST` (default `0.0.0.0`) and port `OWGE_WS_PORT` (default `7474`). It sets `setRandomSession(true)` and installs a `JacksonJsonSupport` backed by a `JavaTimeModule` (so `Instant`/`java.time` payloads serialize properly over the wire).

The server lifecycle lives in `/public/owge/business/src/main/java/com/kevinguanchedarias/owgejava/business/SocketIoService.java`: `@PostConstruct init()` constructs the `SocketIOServer` from the config and registers the unauthenticated event listeners; the server is actually `start()`-ed on `ApplicationReadyEvent`; `@PreDestroy` stops it. The bean is `@DependsOn("bootJacksonConfigurationService")` and holds its own **copy** of the Spring `ObjectMapper` (`mapper = springMapper.copy()`).

On the frontend the client is `socket.io-client` wrapped by `/public/owge/game-frontend/modules/owge-universe/src/lib/services/websocket.service.ts`, with the delta-sync cache in `.../ws-event-cache.service.ts`.

---

## (1) Handshake / authentication flow

Two public constants in `SocketIoService` anchor the flow:
- `AUTHENTICATION = "authentication"` — the event name used both ways (client→server to authenticate, server→client to answer).
- `USER_TOKEN_KEY = "user_token"` — the per-socket store key under which the resolved user identity is stashed on the `SocketIOClient`.

**Connect (unauthenticated).** `registerUnauthenticatedEvents()` adds a connect listener that only logs the remote address. No identity is established at TCP/connect time; an unauthenticated socket can exist but cannot receive game messages (see targeting below).

**Client sends `authentication`.** The frontend (`WebsocketService.authenticate()`) emits, immediately after `connect`:
```js
this._socket.emit('authentication', JSON.stringify({ value: this._credentialsToken, protocol: '0.1.0' }))
```
So the payload is a **JSON string** `{"value": "<jwt>", "protocol": "0.1.0"}`. The `value` is the same JWT the REST API uses (the game session token). The `protocol` field is sent by the client but the server does not read it.

**Server validates the JWT.** The server's `authentication` listener (registered with `String.class`) does:
1. `mapper.readValue(data, Map<String,String>)` and pulls out `"value"`. If empty → `sendError(client, AUTHENTICATION, "invalid token sent from client")`.
2. Otherwise it tries each registered `OwgeJwtAuthenticationFilter` (`@Lazy List<OwgeJwtAuthenticationFilter>` autowired) via `current.findUserFromToken(token)`, taking the first non-null result. `OwgeJwtAuthenticationFilter` (`/public/owge/business/.../filter/OwgeJwtAuthenticationFilter.java`) is a thin subclass of kevinsuite's `JwtAuthenticationFilter`; `findUserFromToken` calls the inherited `decodeTokenIfPossible(jwtToken)` and returns `null` on **any** exception (expired/invalid/tampered token) — i.e. the same JWT secret/decoder used for HTTP auth is reused for the socket. The decoded result is a kevinsuite `TokenUser`.
3. **On success:** the `TokenUser` is bound to the socket with `client.set(USER_TOKEN_KEY, tokenUser)`. This per-socket attribute is what later identifies the socket's owner.
4. The server then builds the **initial sync manifest** and replies on the `authentication` event (see below).
5. **On failure:** `sendError(client, AUTHENTICATION, "Invalid credentials")` — emits an error-status message and **disconnects** the socket.

**`sendError` shape.** `client.sendEvent(event, new WebsocketMessage<>(event, text, "error"))` then `client.disconnect()`. So an auth error arrives as a `WebsocketMessage` with `status: "error"` and `value` being the human string.

**Server's success reply (the manifest).** After binding the user, the server sends back the list of *what events this user has data for and when each was last sent* — this is the seed for delta-sync:
- `eventsInfo = websocketEventsInformationBo.toDto(websocketEventsInformationBo.findByUserId(tokenUser.getId()))` — every `WebsocketEventsInformation` row for the user, as DTOs (`{eventName, userId, lastSent}`).
- It appends one synthetic entry whose `eventName` is `"_universe_id:" + UNIVERSE_ID` (the `UNIVERSE_ID` configuration param). This is **not** a real event; it's a side-channel to ship the universe identifier.
- It emits `client.sendEvent("authentication", new WebsocketMessage<>("authentication", eventsInfo))` → `status: "ok"`, `value: WebsocketEventsInformationDto[]`.

**Client handling of the reply** (`authenticate()` in `websocket.service.ts`): if `status === 'ok'` it loads the user, extracts the universe id from the `_universe_id:` entry (`response.value.find(e => e.eventName.startsWith('_universe_id:'))`), and if it differs from the stored one it **wipes the per-user offline caches** (universe changed). Then `_setupSync(response)` runs delta-sync, marks the socket authenticated, and registers the live message handlers. If `value === 'Invalid credentials'`, the client logs out; any other error rejects. Note the client removes its one-shot `authentication` listener after first response, and re-runs the whole `authenticate()` on every reconnect (guarded by `_isAuthenticated`).

---

## (2) Emitted events — names and payload shapes

**Envelope.** Almost all server→client traffic is wrapped in `WebsocketMessage<T>` (`/public/owge/business/.../pojo/WebsocketMessage.java`) with fields: `eventName: String`, `value: T`, `status: "ok"|"error"`, `lastSent: Instant`. `setStatus` rejects anything but `"ok"`/`"error"`. The `lastSent` is populated only when the message was built from a `WebsocketEventsInformation` row (the normal `sendMessage` path), letting the client advance its per-event watermark.

**Transport-level socketio event names** (the literal first arg to `client.sendEvent`): there are only a handful:
- **`deliver_message`** — the universal carrier for *all* game state-change events. The real domain event name lives **inside** the envelope's `eventName`. This is the central insight: business code "emits `unit_obtained_change`", but on the wire that is `sendEvent("deliver_message", WebsocketMessage{eventName:"unit_obtained_change", ...})`. The frontend's single `_socket.on('deliver_message', ...)` handler dispatches by `message.eventName`.
- **`authentication`** — handshake reply (above).
- **`cache_clear`** — broadcast by `clearCache()` to *every* connected client with literal payload `"null"`; the frontend reacts by running a full `clearCache()` (cache panic recovery / global invalidation).

**Domain event names (the `eventName` inside `deliver_message`)**, gathered from the `*EventEmitter`/`*EventEmitterBo` classes and ad-hoc `sendMessage` callers, with payload type:

| eventName | emitted by | payload (`value`) |
|---|---|---|
| `unit_obtained_change` | `ObtainedUnitEventEmitter.UNIT_OBTAINED_CHANGE` | user's completed obtained units (`obtainedUnitFinderBo.findCompletedAsDto(user)`) |
| `unit_type_change` | `ObtainedUnitEventEmitter.UNIT_TYPE_CHANGE` | `unitTypeBo.findUnitTypesWithUserInfo(userId)` |
| `enemy_mission_change` | `MissionEventEmitterBo.ENEMY_MISSION_CHANGE` | `runningMissionFinderBo.findEnemyRunningMissions(user)` |
| `unit_mission_change` | `MissionEventEmitterBo.UNIT_MISSION_CHANGE` | `MissionWebsocketMessage{count:int, myUnitMissions:UnitRunningMissionDto[]}` |
| `missions_count_change` | `MissionEventEmitterBo.MISSIONS_COUNT_CHANGE` | `Integer` (count of unresolved missions) |
| `unit_build_mission_change` | `MissionEventEmitterBo.emitUnitBuildChange` (const `MissionBo.UNIT_BUILD_MISSION_CHANGE`) | `missionFinderBo.findBuildMissions(userId)` |
| `user_max_energy_change` | `UserEventEmitterBo.USER_MAX_ENERGY_CHANGE` | `userEnergyServiceBo.findMaxEnergy(user)` |
| `user_data_change` | `UserEventEmitterBo.USER_DATA_CHANGE` | `UserStorageDto` (full user: improvements, faction, home planet+galaxy, alliance, consumed/max energy) |
| `user_improvements_change` | `ImprovementBo` | user improvements DTO |

| `warn_message` | `SocketIoService.sendWarning` (`EVENT_WARN_MESSAGE`) | i18n warning string |

Two emitter classes are **internal listener fan-outs, not socketio emitters**: `AttackEventEmitter` (`/public/owge/business/.../mission/attack/AttackEventEmitter.java`) invokes `AfterUnitKilledCalculationListener`/`AfterAttackEndListener` Java listeners during combat math, and `RequirementInternalEventEmitterService` fans out `RequirementComplianceListener.relationObtained/relationLost`. Neither touches the websocket directly — they fire in-process hooks that *may* themselves later cause `sendMessage` calls. They use the "emitter" name by analogy only.

## (3) Per-user rooms / targeting

OWGE does **not** use socketio rooms. Targeting is done by **scanning all connected clients and filtering on the per-socket `USER_TOKEN_KEY` attribute**:

`findClientSockets(int targetUserId)` returns `server.getAllClients()` filtered to clients where `client.get(USER_TOKEN_KEY) != null` **and** (`targetUserId == 0` OR the bound `TokenUser`'s id equals `targetUserId`). Consequences:
- A socket that never authenticated (no `USER_TOKEN_KEY`) is invisible to all targeted sends.
- `targetUserId == 0` is the **broadcast sentinel** — "all authenticated users". `sendMessage(UserStorage user, ...)` with a `null` user collapses to `0` (broadcast).
- A user with multiple tabs has multiple sockets; all matching sockets receive the message (multi-device fan-out).

**Send pipeline** (`sendMessage` → `handleSendMessage`):
1. Resolve target sockets.
2. **Persist the watermark first.** For broadcast (`targetUserId==0`) it iterates `userStorageRepository.findAll()` and saves a `WebsocketEventsInformation(eventName, userId)` per user; otherwise it saves one row for the target. The saved row carries `lastSent` (truncated to seconds). This happens **even if the user is offline**, so the delta-sync watermark is always advanced.
3. `handleSendMessage`: if there are sockets, it lazily computes the payload once (`messageContent.get()`), then `asyncRunnerBo.runAsyncWithoutContext(...)` loops the sockets and `client.sendEvent("deliver_message", new WebsocketMessage<>(savedInformation.get(user.getId()), sendValue))`. The async runner deliberately pushes the actual emission **off the request/transaction thread** (it warns if a transaction is still active — emissions must run after commit). If there are **no** sockets and a `notConnectedAction` was supplied, that runnable runs instead (used to e.g. queue work for offline users).

**`sendOneTimeMessage`** (`@Transactional(NOT_SUPPORTED)`) skips the DB watermark entirely (`savedInformation == null`), so the envelope is built with `new WebsocketMessage<>(eventName, value)` and no `lastSent`. Used for ephemeral, non-synchronizable notifications.

**After-commit discipline.** Emitters that mutate state wrap sends in `transactionUtilService.doAfterCommit(...)` (e.g. `MissionEventEmitterBo.emitLocalMissionChangeAfterCommit`, `ObtainedUnitEventEmitter.emitObtainedUnitsAfterCommit`, `RequirementBo.emitUnlockedChange`, `UserEventEmitterBo`'s improvement listener). Combined with the async runner, this guarantees clients are notified only after the DB row is durably committed.

**Enemy targeting nuance.** `MissionEventEmitterBo.emitEnemyMissionsChange(Mission)` resolves the *target planet's owner* and emits `enemy_mission_change` to that owner — but only if the owner exists and is not the acting user, and (in `emitLocalMissionChange`) only when the mission is not `invisible`. So "who receives" can be a different user than who triggered the action.

---

## (4) `websocket_events_information` and `websocket_messages_status` tables (delta-sync)

### `websocket_events_information` — the delta-sync watermark (actively used)

Schema (`/public/owge/business/database/02_schema.sql` ~L1194): `(event_name VARCHAR(100), user_id INT, last_sent DATETIME)`, PK `(event_name, user_id)`, FK `user_id → user_storage(id)`. Mapped by entity `WebsocketEventsInformation` with `@EmbeddedId EventNameUserId{eventName, userId}` and `lastSent: Instant` (default truncated to seconds). Managed by `WebsocketEventsInformationBo`.

**Role: a per-user, per-event "last changed at" watermark that drives the frontend's offline/delta cache.** The model is deliberately decoupled from payloads:
- Every time the server emits an event for a user (`SocketIoService.sendMessage`), it **upserts** the `(eventName, userId)` row with `last_sent = now` (seconds precision) — `WebsocketEventsInformationBo.save(...)`. This is the canonical "this user's data of type X changed at time T."
- On authentication, the server returns **all** of the user's rows as the manifest (`findByUserId`). The frontend (`ws-event-cache.service.setEventsInformation`) compares each row's `lastSent` against its locally cached value and flags `changed = (no local entry) || (lastSent differs)`.
- `WsEventCacheService.applySync()` then fetches **only the changed, synchronizable** events from the REST endpoint `GET game/websocket-sync?keys=...`, and stores the payloads in per-event IndexedDB/offline stores keyed `ws_cache::<event>`. So the websocket carries *change notifications*; the actual heavy data is pulled over HTTP only when the watermark moved. Live `deliver_message` events also call `saveEventData` to update both the payload store and the watermark, so a connected client stays current without re-fetching.
- The set of events the frontend treats as synchronizable is the hardcoded `_ALLOWED_EVENTS` list in `ws-event-cache.service.ts` (planet_owned_change, running_upgrade_change, enemy_mission_change, unit_type_change, mission_report_change, missions_count_change, unit_mission_change, unit_build_mission_change, obtained_upgrades_change, unit_obtained_change, unit_requirements_change, user_data_change, system_message_change, time_special_change, tutorial_entries_change, upgrade_types_change, unit_unlocked_change, speed_impact_group_unlocked_change, visited_tutorial_entry_change, twitch_state_change). Events not in this list are handled live but not cached as synchronizable.

**Server side of the sync endpoint.** `WebsocketSyncRestService` (`/public/owge/game-rest/.../rest/game/WebsocketSyncRestService.java`, `GET game/websocket-sync?keys=`) delegates to `WebsocketSyncService.findWantedData(keys)`. That service auto-discovers all `SyncSource` beans at startup and merges their `findSyncHandlers()` maps (one lambda per event key; duplicate keys throw `ProgrammingException`). The **SyncSource implementations are the `game-rest` REST controllers themselves** (`UnitRestService`, `MissionRestService`, `UserRestService`, `PlanetRestService`, `ReportRestService`, `TutorialRestService`, `SpeedImpactRestService`, `TwitchStateRestService`, etc.), each registering keys via `SyncHandlerBuilder.create().withHandler("event_name", user -> data).build()`. Registered keys include all the `*_change` events above plus `tutorial_entries_change`, `upgrade_types_change`, `unit_requirements_change`, `unit_unlocked_change`, `twitch_state_change` (`TWITCH_STATE_CHANGE`). For each requested key with a handler, `findWantedData` runs the lambda against the logged-in user, returns `{data, lastSent}`, and **re-saves the watermark** with that timestamp (`websocketEventsInformationBo.save(key, userId, date)`) so the browser and server agree on `lastSent`. Keys with no handler are simply omitted (the frontend then stores them as `data:null`).

**Watermark maintenance / lifecycle.** `clearCache()` (server) does **not** delete rows; it calls `WebsocketEventsInformationBo.clear()` which bumps every user's `last_sent` to `now` (forcing all clients to treat everything as changed) and emits the `cache_clear` socketio event to all clients. User deletion removes the rows (`doDeleteUser` → `deleteByEventNameUserIdUserId`, also mirrored in the MySQL procedure `05_mysql_procedures.sql` `DELETE FROM websocket_events_information WHERE user_id = ...`). The `_universe_id:` manifest entry is explicitly skipped by the frontend when computing `changed`.

**Hardening around the sync endpoint.** Because `game/websocket-sync` resolves and serializes a large slice of user state, it is protected by `WebsocketSyncRateLimitFilter` (per-IP, fixed 60s window, default 10 req/min via `OWGE_WS_SYNC_RATELIMIT_PER_MINUTE`, `429` beyond that; IP from trusted proxy header `X-OWGE-RMT-IP` or `getRemoteAddr`). The frontend complements this with reconnect exponential backoff (1s→30s) and a `_MIN_RESYNC_INTERVAL_MS = 10000` resync throttle, plus a desync-recovery path (`refetchEvent`) for when the watermark claims fresh but the offline store is empty (typical after a backend restart). The `open/websocket-sync/{event}` variants (`rule_change`, `speed_group_change`) are cached/cheap and **not** rate-limited.

### `websocket_messages_status` — legacy / unused

Schema (~L1206): `(id BIGINT UNSIGNED PK auto-increment, user_id INT FK→user_storage, event_name VARCHAR(100), unwhiling_to_delivery TINYINT, socket_server_ack TINYINT, socket_not_found TINYINT, web_browser_ack TINYTEXT, is_user_ack_required TINYTEXT, user_ack DATETIME)`. The column set sketches an intended **per-message delivery/ack ledger** (server ack, socket-not-found, browser ack, user-required-ack with timestamp). **However, a full-repo search for the table name, a matching JPA entity/repository, and every distinctive column (`unwhiling_to_delivery`, `socket_server_ack`, `web_browser_ack`, etc.) across `.java` and `.ts` returns nothing.** There is no entity, no repository, no read/write of it anywhere in `business`, `game-rest`, or the frontend. It is a **dead/legacy table** — schema-only, presumably a planned reliable-delivery/ack mechanism that was never wired up. The actual delivery model is fire-and-forget over `deliver_message` with no per-message acknowledgement; reliability is achieved instead by the `websocket_events_information` watermark + HTTP re-sync, which lets a client that missed live events catch up on reconnect.

# OWGE Mission System — Reference for Rust Reimplementation

## 1. Overview and the two scheduler worlds

OWGE's core game loop is a *delayed-action* system: a player action (build a unit, research an upgrade, send a fleet) is persisted as a `Mission` row with a future `termination_date`, and an asynchronous worker fires it when due, runs the type-specific logic, mutates game state, and pushes the result to clients over websocket.

There are **two distinct families** of missions, and they are dispatched through *different* top-level Bos:

- **Non-unit ("local") missions** — `BUILD_UNIT` and `LEVEL_UP`. Logic lives in `MissionBo` (`business/MissionBo.java`). These operate on a single planet/user, not on fleets moving between planets.
- **Unit missions** — `EXPLORE`, `GATHER`, `ESTABLISH_BASE`, `ATTACK`, `COUNTERATTACK`, `CONQUEST`, `DEPLOY`, `RETURN_MISSION`. Logic lives in `UnitMissionBo` (`business/UnitMissionBo.java`) which dispatches to a per-type `MissionProcessor` strategy implementation.

`MissionType` (`enumerations/MissionType.java`) carries an integer `value` and `isUnitMission()` returns true for values 4–11 (`EXPLORE` through `DEPLOY`). `RETURN_MISSION` (value 5) is a unit mission; `LEVEL_UP`(1)/`BROADCAST_MESSAGE`(2)/`BUILD_UNIT`(3)/`DEPLOYED`(12) are not. Note `DEPLOYED` is a terminal *state* of stationed units, not a fired mission.

The asynchronous engine is **db-scheduler** (`com.github.kagkarlsson`), a library that polls a SQL table for due tasks. A separate **Quartz** scheduler also exists for unrelated periodic jobs — do not conflate them. For the Rust port, "the mission loop" means the db-scheduler half only.

## 2. db-scheduler configuration and the `scheduled_tasks` table

Configured in `game-rest/src/main/resources/application.properties`:

```
db-scheduler.enabled=true
db-scheduler.heartbeat-interval=5m
db-scheduler.polling-interval=3s
db-scheduler.table-name=scheduled_tasks
db-scheduler.immediate-execution-enabled=true
db-scheduler.scheduler-name=OWGE_BACKGROUND
db-scheduler.threads=10
db-scheduler.delay-startup-until-context-ready=false
db-scheduler.polling-strategy=fetch
db-scheduler.polling-strategy-lower-limit-fraction-of-threads=0.5
db-scheduler.polling-strategy-upper-limit-fraction-of-threads=3.0
db-scheduler.shutdown-max-wait=30m
```

The table (`business/database/02_schema.sql:795`, InnoDB):

```sql
CREATE TABLE `scheduled_tasks` (
  `task_name` varchar(40) NOT NULL,        -- always 'mission-run' here
  `task_instance` varchar(40) NOT NULL,    -- the Mission.id as a string
  `task_data` blob,                        -- null for these tasks (no payload)
  `execution_time` timestamp(6) NOT NULL,  -- when it becomes due
  `picked` tinyint(1) NOT NULL,            -- claimed-by-a-worker flag
  `picked_by` varchar(50) DEFAULT NULL,    -- scheduler instance id
  `last_success` timestamp(6) NULL,
  `last_failure` timestamp(6) NULL,
  `consecutive_failures` int DEFAULT NULL,
  `last_heartbeat` timestamp(6) NULL,      -- liveness for crash recovery
  `version` bigint NOT NULL                -- optimistic-lock counter
);
ALTER TABLE `scheduled_tasks`
  ADD PRIMARY KEY (`task_name`,`task_instance`),
  ADD KEY `execution_time_idx` (`execution_time`),
  ADD KEY `last_heartbeat_idx` (`last_heartbeat`);
```

**What db-scheduler does (the behavior a Rust port must replicate):**
- A pool of up to 10 worker threads polls every 3s (`fetch` strategy with the lower/upper fraction limits controlling when it prefetches more) for rows whose `execution_time <= now()` and `picked = 0`.
- It atomically *claims* a due row by setting `picked = 1`, `picked_by = <instance>`, `last_heartbeat = now()`, and bumping `version` (optimistic concurrency via the `version` column — the `UPDATE ... WHERE version = ?` is what prevents two instances from running the same task). Multiple app instances against one DB cooperate safely this way.
- While running, the worker periodically updates `last_heartbeat` (every 5m). If a worker dies mid-execution, another instance detects a stale heartbeat (older than the heartbeat interval window) and re-claims the task — this is the crash-recovery story.
- A `oneTime` task is **deleted** from the table on success; on exception it is **rescheduled** (left in the table with a new `execution_time`) per the failure handler. Here, OWGE supplies its *own* retry logic (see §6), so the realization job catches exceptions itself rather than relying on db-scheduler's retry.
- `immediate-execution-enabled=true` means when a task is scheduled with an `execution_time` already in the past (or essentially now), the scheduling thread can hand it straight to a worker without waiting for the next poll.

The task is declared in `DbSchedulerConfiguration.java`:

```java
Tasks.oneTime(new TaskWithoutDataDescriptor("mission-run"))
     .execute((instance, ctx) -> dbSchedulerRealizationJob.execute(Long.parseLong(instance.getId())));
```

So `task_name = "mission-run"`, `task_instance = mission.getId().toString()`, no payload — **the mission id is encoded as the task instance string, and the row carries no other data.** Everything else is re-read from the `mission` table at fire time.

## 3. Lifecycle: register → schedule → fire → process → resolve

### Register (synchronous, inside the HTTP request transaction)
A REST controller calls a `*register*` method. The Bo:
1. Validates ownership/limits/requirements (e.g. `planetCheckerService.myCheckIsOfUserProperty`, `missionBaseService.checkMissionLimitNotReached`).
2. Computes resource cost and required time, subtracts resources from the user.
3. Builds a `Mission` entity: `startingDate = now (UTC)`, `type`, `user`, `requiredTime`, `terminationDate = now + requiredTime` (see §5), plus a `MissionInformation` holding type-specific data (e.g. for `BUILD_UNIT` the `value` field stores the planet id as a double; for `LEVEL_UP` it stores the target level and an `ObjectRelation` pointing at the upgrade).
4. Saves the mission (and for builds, an `ObtainedUnit` row linked to the mission).
5. Calls `missionSchedulerService.scheduleMission(mission)` to enqueue the db-scheduler row.
6. Registers `transactionUtilService.doAfterCommit(...)` callbacks that refresh entities and emit websocket events **after the DB commit**.

### Schedule (`MissionSchedulerService.java`)
```java
scheduler.schedule(
    BASIC_ONE_TIME_TASK.instance(mission.getId().toString()),
    Instant.now().plusSeconds(mission.getRequiredTime().longValue() - DELAY_HANDLE));  // DELAY_HANDLE = 2
```
The `execution_time` is `now + requiredTime − 2 seconds`. The 2s shave is a small lead so the mission fires right around its `termination_date`. Both schedule and cancel run with `@Transactional(propagation = NOT_SUPPORTED)` — they deliberately operate **outside** the surrounding transaction so the scheduler's own writes are independent. `abortMissionJob` cancels by deleting the row (`scheduler.cancel(instance)`), tolerating `TaskInstanceNotFoundException` if already gone.

> Important ordering subtlety for the Rust port: the mission row and the `scheduled_tasks` row are written in the same logical action but the scheduler write is non-transactional w.r.t. the mission. The design relies on `execution_time` being seconds in the future, so by the time a worker could pick it up the mission's own transaction has committed. A naive Rust port that inserts the schedule row inside the same transaction as the mission is actually *safer* and is the recommended simplification.

### Fire (`DbSchedulerRealizationJob.execute(Long missionId)`)
This is the single entry point a worker invokes. It:
1. Renames the thread `OWGE_BACKGROUND_<missionId>` (for log correlation).
2. `transactionUtilService.clearStatus()` — resets thread-local lock/transaction bookkeeping (the MySQL named-lock state is thread-local; see §7).
3. Loads the mission; if it's `null` or already `resolved`, does nothing (idempotency guard — a re-fired or duplicate task is a no-op).
4. Dispatches by type:
   - `BUILD_UNIT` / `LEVEL_UP` → `missionBo.runMission(missionId, type)`.
   - everything else → `unitMissionBo.runUnitMission(missionId, type)`.
5. On **any exception**, calls `missionBaseService.retryMissionIfPossible(...)` (§6), re-emits the relevant websocket channel so clients see consistent state, and if it was a `PessimisticLockingFailureException` dumps InnoDB status + process list for diagnostics.

### Process (type-specific)
For unit missions, `UnitMissionBo.runUnitMission` (see §4) acquires planet locks then calls `doRunUnitMission`, which runs interception logic and then the matching `MissionProcessor.process(mission, involvedUnits)`. Each processor mutates state, registers follow-up missions (notably a `RETURN_MISSION` for the survivors), builds a report, and sets `mission.setResolved(true)`.

For `BUILD_UNIT`/`LEVEL_UP`, `MissionBo.processBuildUnit` / `processLevelUpAnUpgrade` apply the effect (move built units onto the planet / raise the upgrade level) and **delete** the mission row.

### Resolve (two different terminal conventions — note this!)
- **Unit missions are "resolved", not deleted**: the processor sets `resolved = true` and the row stays. A nightly job `MissionBo.deleteOldMissions` (`@Scheduled cron 0 0 2 * * *`, also run once on `ApplicationReadyEvent`) purges resolved missions older than 60 days, first null-ing `relatedMission` back-links.
- **`BUILD_UNIT`/`LEVEL_UP` missions are deleted outright** at process time.

The db-scheduler `scheduled_tasks` row is removed by db-scheduler itself when `execute` returns normally (oneTime success). So "resolved + row gone from scheduled_tasks" is the success terminal state; the `mission` row's lifetime is governed separately.

## 4. UnitMissionBo dispatch and the processor strategy

`UnitMissionBo` autowires `List<MissionProcessor>` and, in `@PostConstruct init()`, builds `Map<MissionType, MissionProcessor>` by asking each processor `supports(type)` for every unit mission type. The `MissionProcessor` interface (`processor/MissionProcessor.java`):

```java
boolean supports(MissionType missionType);
UnitMissionReportBuilder process(Mission mission, List<ObtainedUnit> involvedUnits);
```

All processors are `@Transactional(propagation = MANDATORY)` — they must run inside an already-open transaction (the one `runUnitMission` opens), never start their own.

`runUnitMission` (the dispatch core):
```java
@Transactional(isolation = READ_COMMITTED)
@Retryable(retryFor = CannotAcquireLockException.class,
           backoff = @Backoff(delay = 500, random = true, maxDelay = 750, multiplier = 2))
public void runUnitMission(Long missionId, MissionType missionType) {
    transactionUtilService.clearStatus();
    var mission = findByIdOrDie(...);
    planetLockUtilService.doInsideLock(resolvePlanetsToLock(mission),
        () -> doRunUnitMission(mission, missionType));
}
```

`doRunUnitMission`:
1. `missionInterceptionManagerBo.loadInformation(mission, type)` — checks whether enemy "interceptor" units (by speed-impact-group) intercept the incoming fleet. If **all** involved units are intercepted, the mission is fully intercepted: it's marked resolved, a report is sent, intercepted units deleted, and no processor runs. Otherwise partially-intercepted units are deleted and the survivor list is recomputed. `RETURN_MISSION` is never intercepted.
2. If not fully intercepted, calls the type's processor with the surviving `involvedUnits`.
3. Appends interception data to the report and saves it via `missionReportManagerBo`.

### Per-type processor summaries
- **ExploreMissionProcessor** — marks the target planet explored for the user, snapshots the units present on it for the report, registers a return mission, resolves.
- **GatherMissionProcessor** — optionally triggers an attack first (`triggerAttackIfRequired`); if the fleet survives, computes gathered resources = Σ(unit.charge × count) × planet richness × (1 + moreChargeCapacity improvement), split primary/secondary by faction percentages (default 50/50), credits the user, registers a return mission, emits a `mission_gather_result` socket event, resolves.
- **EstablishBaseMissionProcessor** — optional attack trigger; if target has an owner or user is at planet cap, fails and returns the fleet; otherwise `definePlanetAsOwnedBy`. Resolves.
- **AttackMissionProcessor** — the combat path (§8). Builds `AttackInformation`, runs `startAttack`, registers a return mission for survivors unless the attack mission's own units were all wiped (`isRemoved`), resolves. Also exposes `triggerAttackIfRequired` used by gather/establish/conquest when `MISSION_<TYPE>_TRIGGER_ATTACK` config is true.
- **CounterattackMissionProcessor** — delegates straight to `AttackMissionProcessor.process`.
- **ConquestMissionProcessor** — runs `processAttack` (no auto-return), then decides if the old owner *and* their alliance are fully defeated; if so and the user isn't at planet cap and it isn't a home planet, transfers planet ownership (`definePlanetAsOwnedBy`), cancels any in-progress build mission on that planet, and notifies the old owner; otherwise registers a return mission with a failure report.
- **DeployMissionProcessor** — moves the fleet onto the target planet to be stationed there (becomes a `DEPLOYED`-state holding), sets invisibility if all units are hidden, resolves. No return mission.
- **ReturnMissionProcessor** — moves the units back onto the source planet, resolves, asynchronously re-triggers unit requirement checks and emits obtained-unit updates. Return missions cannot be cancelled by players.

### Mission cancellation
`UnitMissionBo.myCancelMission` doesn't delete an in-flight unit mission; it marks it resolved and immediately registers a **return mission** for the *remaining* travel time (`requiredTime − elapsed`), so cancelled fleets fly home rather than vanish. `MissionBo.cancelMission` (for build/level-up) refunds resources (or, for builds, delegates to `missionCancelBuildService`), deletes the mission, and calls `abortMissionJob` to remove the `scheduled_tasks` row.

## 5. Delay / required-time computation

Two regimes:

**Build/level-up (`MissionBo`)** — `requiredTime` comes from the resource-requirements calculation, then is scaled by the user's build/research-speed improvement (`improvementBo.computeImprovementValue`). Dev shortcuts: config `ZERO_BUILD_TIME` / `ZERO_UPGRADE_TIME` (default `"TRUE"`) force `requiredTime = 3` seconds. `terminationDate = computeTerminationDate(requiredTime)`.

**Unit missions (`MissionTimeManagerBo.java`)** — base time per type from `missionConfigurationBo.findMissionBaseTimeByType(type)`. Then `handleMissionTimeCalculation` adds a travel penalty based on fleet speed and map distance, *unless* every unit is in a "fixed" speed-impact group (then base time stands):

```
requiredTime = baseTime + ((leftMultiplier × moveCost) × (100 − slowestUnitSpeedWithImprovement)) / divisor
requiredTime = max(baseTime, requiredTime)
```
where:
- **slowest unit speed** = the minimum `unit.speed` among non-fixed units, boosted by that unit type's SPEED improvement for the user.
- **leftMultiplier** ("mission penalty") = config `MISSION_SPEED_<TYPE>_{SAME_Q|DIFF_Q|DIFF_S|DIFF_G}` chosen by comparing source/target galaxy, sector, quadrant. Defaults: same quadrant 50, diff quadrant 100, diff sector 200, diff galaxy 2000.
- **moveCost** = weighted map distance: `|planetNumberΔ|×P_MOVE_COST + |quadrantΔ|×Q_MOVE_COST + |sectorΔ|×S_MOVE_COST + (galaxy differs ? G_MOVE_COST : 0)` with default per-step costs 0.01 / 0.02 / 0.03 / 0.15, all overridable via `MISSION_SPEED_<TYPE>_*_MOVE_COST` config.
- **divisor** = config `MISSION_SPEED_DIVISOR_<TYPE>` (default 1, treated as 1 if 0).

`handleCustomDuration` lets a caller force a longer duration. `computeTerminationDate(requiredTime) = now(UTC).plusSeconds(requiredTime.intValue())`. **All times are UTC.** Return missions reuse the origin mission's `requiredTime` (or a custom shorter value when cancelling mid-flight).

## 6. Failure handling and retries — two layered mechanisms

1. **Lock-contention retry** (`@Retryable` on `runUnitMission`): a `CannotAcquireLockException` (thrown when MySQL named locks can't be obtained, §7) retries the *whole* transaction with randomized exponential backoff (500ms base, ×2, capped ~750ms).

2. **Application mission retry** (`MissionBaseService.retryMissionIfPossible`, `MAX_ATTEMPTS = 3`): invoked by the realization job's catch block on any other exception. If the mission's `attemps` counter is below 3, it increments `attemps`, recomputes `terminationDate`, saves an error report, and **re-schedules** via `missionSchedulerService.scheduleMission` (a fresh `scheduled_tasks` row). On the 3rd failure it gives up cleanly per type: unit missions register a return mission and mark resolved; `BUILD_UNIT` deletes the obtained units and the mission; `LEVEL_UP` deletes the mission. The `mission.attemps` column persists the attempt count across fires.

A Rust port needs both: a transient-lock retry around the locked critical section, and a persisted per-mission attempt counter with a hard cap and type-specific give-up semantics.

## 7. MySQL named locks serialize per-planet / per-user work

Concurrency is controlled with **MySQL application-level named locks** (`GET_LOCK`/`RELEASE_LOCK`), not row locks, implemented in `MysqlLockUtilService.java`. Wrappers build the key strings:
- `PlanetLockUtilService` → `planet_lock_<planetId>`
- `UserLockUtilService` → `user_lock_<userId>`

Key properties to reproduce:
- **Thread-local tracking** of already-held keys (`MysqlLockState`). If a requested key is already held by this thread, the body runs without re-locking (re-entrant fast path). This is why the realization job calls `clearStatus()` first — the worker thread is pooled and reused.
- **Deadlock avoidance by global ordering**: locks are always acquired in **sorted (ascending) order across the whole union** of currently-held + newly-wanted keys. To guarantee this even under nesting, it *releases* what it holds and re-takes the entire union sorted. (When a caller pre-acquires the full superset it will ever need, the fast path skips this entirely — see `UnitMissionBo.resolvePlanetsToLock` below.)
- **Timeout + bounded retry**: each `GET_LOCK` waits `TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10`; up to `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS = 5` attempts (with deadlock-detection retries and a 200ms sleep when only a subset was acquired). On final failure it logs InnoDB status/process list and **throws `CannotAcquireLockException`** — it deliberately does *not* run the protected body unlocked (a prior bug that corrupted state).
- **Release timing**: if a transaction is active, release is deferred to `doAfterCompletion` (so the lock is held for the whole transaction); otherwise released immediately in a `finally`. Each frame releases only the keys *it* introduced; outer-frame keys remain owned by the outer frame.

`UnitMissionBo.resolvePlanetsToLock(mission)` acquires, up front, the source and target planets **plus every planet owned by their owners** in one sorted acquisition. The reason: processing re-evaluates unit requirements which internally locks *all* of a user's planets; acquiring that superset first means the nested requirement check finds its keys already held and skips a second, possibly out-of-order `GET_LOCK` — exactly the inversion that caused production deadlocks. The registration paths (`MissionBo.registerBuildUnit`, `registerLevelUpAnUpgrade`) additionally take a **per-user** lock around check-then-insert to close cross-planet uniqueness races (e.g. building the same unique unit on two planets concurrently, or starting two `LEVEL_UP`s).

**For Rust**: MySQL `GET_LOCK` is a session-scoped advisory lock — the same connection that takes it must release it (or hold the connection for the locked region). A Rust port using a connection pool must pin one connection for the duration of a locked critical section, or replace this with an equivalent serialization primitive (Postgres advisory locks `pg_advisory_lock(key)`, a Redis lock, or an in-process keyed mutex if single-instance). The *semantics* to preserve: keyed mutual exclusion on `planet:<id>` and `user:<id>`, re-entrant per task execution, acquired in a globally consistent order, with timeout → retryable error.

## 8. Combat math (`AttackMissionManagerBo.java`)

`AttackMissionProcessor.processAttack` → `buildAttackInformation` → `startAttack`. Combat:

1. **Gather combatants** (`buildAttackInformation`): all units "involved in attack" present on the target planet (excluding the attacker's own mission units, which are added separately) plus the attack mission's units, grouped per user into `AttackUserInformation`.
2. **Setup** (`startAttack`): shuffle the unit list (randomized targeting order); for each user, compute its `attackableUnits` = all units owned by an **enemy** (`allianceBo.areEnemies` — alliance-aware, so allies don't fight each other).
3. **Resolve** (`doAttack`): for each attacker unit, filter targets it *can* attack via attack rules (`unit.attackRule` or the type's default rule; `attackRuleBo.canAttack`), score each target by **critical multiplier** (`findCriticalScore`; default 1.0, or the matching critical-attack entry's value vs the target's unit type), sort targets by score descending, and attack in that order until the attacker's pending attack is exhausted (`noAttack`).
4. **`attackTarget`** — per engagement:
   - `myAttack = pendingAttack × criticalScore`.
   - Shield bypass: if the attacker bypasses shields (`attackBypassShieldService`), it ignores the target's shield pool; `victimHealth = health` (else `health + shield`).
   - **Kill count** (`addPointsAndUpdateCount`): `healthPerUnit = totalHealth(+totalShield)/initialCount`; `killed = floor(myAttack / healthPerUnit)`, clamped to remaining count; attacker earns `killed × victimUnit.points`.
   - If the target group **survives** the volley (`victimHealth > myAttack`): attacker's pending attack goes to 0 (`noAttack`); damage is split half to shield, half to health (or all to health if bypassing); negative shield overflow rolls into health.
   - If the target group is **destroyed** (`victimHealth <= myAttack`): attacker keeps leftover attack (`myAttack − victimHealth`, never exceeding its original value) to hit the next target; the target's `ObtainedUnit` row is deleted, holder-unit references are unset (units stored inside a destroyed carrier are freed), and if that was the last unit of a mission, the mission is deleted (`deleteMissionIfRequired`) — if it was the *attack* mission's own units, `attackInformation.setRemoved(true)` instead (so no return mission is registered).
5. **Persist + notify** (`updatePoints` / tail of `startAttack`): award accumulated points to each user, persist surviving unit counts (`obtainedUnitBo.saveWithChange(unit, -killed)`), then after commit emit obtained-unit changes, mission-list changes, and user-data updates to every affected user and the planet owner; finally `attackEventEmitter.emitAttackEnd`.

Combat mutation happens **inside** the planet-locked transaction opened by `runUnitMission`, so concurrent attacks on the same planet are serialized by the `planet_lock_<id>` named locks.

## 9. What a Rust port needs

1. **A db-scheduler equivalent.** A worker pool that, on an interval (~3s), polls `scheduled_tasks` for `execution_time <= now() AND picked = 0`, atomically claims rows via an optimistic `UPDATE ... SET picked, picked_by, last_heartbeat, version+1 WHERE version = ?` (multi-instance safe), runs `execute(mission_id)`, deletes the row on success, and recovers crashed tasks via stale `last_heartbeat`. Mission id is the only payload (the `task_instance` string). Crates: a hand-rolled poller over `sqlx`, or evaluate existing job-queue crates — but the optimistic-version claim + heartbeat recovery semantics are the load-bearing parts to preserve. Keep `execution_time = now + requiredTime − 2s` and idempotency (skip if mission missing or already `resolved`).

2. **A dispatch layer** = `DbSchedulerRealizationJob`: load mission, guard resolved/missing, branch build/level-up vs unit, catch-all → app-level retry with a persisted `attemps` counter (cap 3) and type-specific give-up, plus per-channel websocket re-emit on failure.

3. **The processor strategy** keyed by `MissionType`, each a transactional unit that mutates state, may register a follow-up `RETURN_MISSION`, builds a report, and sets `resolved = true`. Plus the interception pre-pass.

4. **Keyed serialization** replacing MySQL named locks: per-`planet` and per-`user` mutual exclusion, re-entrant within one execution, globally ordered acquisition (sort keys), timeout → retryable error, and an up-front superset acquisition for unit missions (source/target planets + all planets of both owners). If single-instance, an in-process keyed mutex map suffices; if multi-instance, use Postgres advisory locks or Redis. The transient-lock error must trigger a backoff retry of the whole locked transaction.

5. **Time/delay computation** per §5 (UTC throughout, config-driven multipliers, slowest-unit-speed travel model, dev zero-time shortcuts).

6. **Two terminal conventions**: unit missions stay as `resolved=true` rows purged after 60 days by a nightly job; build/level-up rows are deleted at completion. The `scheduled_tasks` row is removed on successful execution regardless.

7. **Post-commit event emission**: every state mutation that notifies clients must fire its websocket events *after* the DB transaction commits (the `doAfterCommit` pattern), not inside it.
